coredata.dc:title: High-performance zinc-ion hydrogel electrolytes based on molecular-level hybridization of PVA with polymer quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Jingyu
Zhan, Xiao
Xu, Yuhuan
Diao, Kunlan
Zhang, Daohai
Qin, Shuhao
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous zinc ion batteries have received widespread attention. However, the growth of zinc dendrites and hydrogen evolution reaction generation seriously hinder the practical application of zinc ion batteries. Herein, it is reported that a multifunctional dendrites-free low-temperature PVA-based gel electrolyte by introducing negatively charged polymer carbon quantum dots (QDs) and the organic antifreeze dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) into it. The QDs carrying a large number of functional groups on the surface can effectively adsorb Zn2+, eliminating the “tip effect”, and inducing the uniform deposition of Zn2+ and the formation of a dendrites-free structure. Meanwhile, the solvation structure of adsorbed Zn2+ can be controlled by charged groups to reduce the generation of side reactions, thus obtaining high-performance zinc ion batteries. The Zn/polyaniline (PANi) full battery can be stably cycled more than 1000 times at –20 °C, and the design of this gel electrolyte can provide good feasibility for safe, stable, and flexible energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solid-state zinc-ion batteries
PVA-based hydrogel
Dendrite-free
Quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Project (No. 52163001), the Guizhou Minzu University Research Platform Grant (No. GZMUGCZX[2021]01), the Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Program Project Grant (Qiankehe Platform Talents-CXTD[2021]005, Qiankehe Platform Talents-GCC[2022]010-1, Qiankehe Fuqi [2023]001), the Central Guided Local Science and Technology Development Funds Project (Qiankehe Zhong Yindi [2023]035), and the Doctor Startup Fund of Guizhou Minzu University (Grant No. GZMUZK [2024] QD77).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process and its hybrid membrane of QDP.

(a) TEM image of QDs. SEM images of the top surface (b–d) and cross-section (e–g) morphology of the as-prepared PP, DP, and QDP hydrogel electrolyte films, respectively. Photographs of the (h) PP and (i) QDP. 2 M ZnSO4 solution with (j) and without (k) DMSO. Top: Room temperature. Bottom: −20 °C. (l) Photograph of QDP under 365 nm UV. (m) Stretching and (n) twisting a QDP strip at −20 °C.

(a) FTIR spectra, (b) full-survey XPS spectra, high resolution (c) S 2p, (d) N 1s, and (e) C 1s XPS spectra of as-prepared QDs powder. (f) Zeta potential of QDs with varying additive amounts. (g) FTIR spectra, (h) stress-strain curves, and (i) DSC curves of PP, DP, and QDP hydrogels before immersing in zinc salt solution.

(a) LSV curves at 1 mV s−1 of SS/SS@cell and (b) EIS spectra of SS/SS@cell at room temperature using PP, DP, and QDP hydrogel electrolyte films. (c) Chronoamperometry curves of QDP-SS/SS@cell. (d) Rate performance and (e) voltage-time profiles of symmetric cells. (f) Charge-discharge curves at 0.1 C. (g) Rate capability. (h) Capacity retentions vs. current density. (i) Long cycle diagram at a current density of 2 C, and (j) charge-discharge curves of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at different cycles.

(a) QDs mechanism diagram. SEM images in cross-section, 50 cycle surface, 1000 cycle surface, 2000 cycle surface of (b) PP-Zn/PANi@cell zinc electrode, (c) DP-Zn/PANi@cell zinc electrode, and (d) QDP-Zn/ PANi@cell zinc electrode.

(a) Rate performance of the batteries at −10 °C and (b) 35 °C. (c) Long cycle diagram at 35 °C and (d) charge/discharge curves of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at different cycles. (e) Long cycle diagram at −20 °C and (f) charge/discharge curves of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at different cycles.

head.ce:title: High-performance zinc-ion hydrogel electrolytes based on molecular-level hybridization of PVA with polymer quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering of Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering of Guizhou Minzu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Engineering of Guizhou Minzu University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Compounding and Modification of Polymer Materials, Guiyang 550014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Compounding and Modification of Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNational Engineering Research Center for Compounding and Modification of Polymer Materials, Guiyang 550014, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous zinc ion batteries have received widespread attention. However, the growth of zinc dendrites and hydrogen evolution reaction generation seriously hinder the practical application of zinc ion batteries. Herein, it is reported that a multifunctional dendrites-free low-temperature PVA-based gel electrolyte by introducing negatively charged polymer carbon quantum dots (QDs) and the organic antifreeze dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) into it. The QDs carrying a large number of functional groups on the surface can effectively adsorb Zn2+, eliminating the “tip effect”, and inducing the uniform deposition of Zn2+ and the formation of a dendrites-free structure. Meanwhile, the solvation structure of adsorbed Zn2+ can be controlled by charged groups to reduce the generation of side reactions, thus obtaining high-performance zinc ion batteries. The Zn/polyaniline (PANi) full battery can be stably cycled more than 1000 times at –20 °C, and the design of this gel electrolyte can provide good feasibility for safe, stable, and flexible energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable zinc ion batteries (ZIBs) have recently emerged as a promising candidate for grid-scale energy storage and portable electronic application due to their ease of manufacturing, abundant resources, intrinsic safety, environmental benignity, and competitive specific capacity (820 mAh g–1 and 5855 mAh cm–3) [1–5]. However, zinc metal anodes still suffer from significant challenges, such as uncontrolled dendrites growth, volume expansion, and corrosion/hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) during the continuous zinc plating/stripping processes, thus ultimately resulting in increased impedance, decreased capacity, and even electrolyte leakage [6–9]. So far, massive efforts have been devoted to tackling the aforementioned bottlenecks through various strategies, including regulation of interface, development of novel electrolytes, introduction of additives, and modification of separator [10–12]. Among these strategies, constructing a Zn-electrolyte interface has been proven to be a relatively feasible and effective approach to decrease the “tip effect” and uniformize the electric field distribution near the interfaces [13,14].

Hydrogel materials with excellent design-ability and tunability are considered the ideal candidates for the electrolyte materials of various energy storage devices, such as rechargeable batteries and supercapacitors. More importantly, adding organic solvents (i.g., dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) [15] or ethylene glycol [16]) or concentrated salts (e.g., acetate [17] or triflate [18], or perchlorate [19]), and polymer modification (e.g., PAA [20] or SA [21]) endow with great anti-freezing property so that it can endure extreme low temperature for the space exploration and high latitude/altitude applications [22]. Recently, the utilization of Poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) hydrogel electrolyte membranes has garnered substantial attention as a capable electrode-electrolyte interface, as they can efficiently impede dendrites formation and mitigate undesired side reactions through the precise management of concentration distribution near the electrode surface [23,24]. Furthermore, the hydrophilic nature of PVA chains allows for the facile formation of hydrogen or/and other chemical bonds with the active sites of diverse organic/inorganic fillers [25] (e.g., metal oxides [26], MOF [27], and GO [28]), thereby facilitating the coupling of properties between these fillers. However, the large size (typically > 100 nm) and weak chemical selectivity of these fillers can disrupt the aggregation of polymer chains, leading to a random distribution within the matrix [29,30]. Consequently, a significant decrement in mechanical strength is observed. As expected, precisely controlling the aggregation structure of the PVA polymers and the distribution of fillers is highly desirable to further understand their effect on the electrochemical performance.

Quantum dots (QDs) have raised worldwide concerns in the field of composite materials due to excellent chemical stability, low toxicity, and cost-effectiveness [31,32]. The captivating feature lies in the molecular-scale magnitude (2–10 nm) of QDs, rendering them exceptional contenders for achieving molecular-level hybridization [33]. Herein, we synthesized the negatively charged QDs through a simple and efficient solvent-thermal method (Fig. 1 ) and subsequently incorporated them into a PVA matrix (QDP). The presence of dangling oxygen-containing groups on QDs enables the modulation of the Zn-electrolyte interface electric field distribution, resulting in an abundance of nucleation sites for the uniform deposition of Zn2+. Thus, the symmetric cell using QDP electrolytes presents outstanding long-term cycling stability over 300 h at a current density of 1.0 mA cm–2 with a fixed capacity of 1.0 mAh cm–2. In addition, the QDP is compatible with the polyaniline (PANi) cathode and can achieve promising properties at low temperatures (–20 °C) with high reversible capacity (e.g., 61.1 mAh g–1 at 0.5 A g–1) and excellent long-term cycling stability (e.g., over 1000 cycles at 0.5 A g–1 with an initial capacity retention of 73.0 %).

We used physical cross-linking to prepare three types of hydrogel electrolytes: gel electrolytes with QDs and DMSO (QDP), gel electrolytes with DMSO (DP), and pure PVA electrolytes (PP). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image reveals that the as-prepared QDs display a typical size of 5–10 nm (Fig. 2 (a)). In addition, the Tyndall effect was also found in the QDs solution, indicating well dispersibility in deionized water. In order to maintain the ratio of DMSO to water in DP and QDP electrolytes, a corresponding percentage of DMSO was added to the solvent of the electrolyte to ensure that the low-temperature performance of the hydrogel would not be affected by the increase of water content after soaking in the electrolyte. The morphology of the cross-section and surface of hydrogel electrolyte films (PP, DP, and QDP) were inspected by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The surface SEM images of pristine hydrogel films (Fig. S1(a–c) in Supporting Information) reveal a distinct dense and smooth bulk pattern in comparison to PP, DP, and QDP (Fig. 2(b–d)) with increasing grain boundaries after immersing in the 2 M ZnSO4 solution. The addition of DMSO and QDs resulted in higher crystallinity and stronger interactions, which led to denser chain segments of the molecules and made the surface of the hydrogel electrolyte smoother. This can also be confirmed from XRD (Fig. S2), at 2θ = 19.7°, there is a diffraction peak, corresponding to the (101) plane of PVA, compared to PP, DP, and QDP, the (101) plane diffraction peak of PVA is shifted to the right, indicating that the addition of DMSO makes the binding between molecular chains in PVA tighter, The diffraction peak at 2θ = 43.8° was attributed to the (102) plane of PVA and the strongest peak intensity was shown in QDP, indicating that the addition of QDs strengthened the hydrogen bond network in the gel electrolyte, improved the interaction force between molecular chains, and effectively improved the crystallinity.

The cross-section SEM images demonstrate that the PP and DP films (Fig. 2(e, f)) show a loose and stochastic structure, in contrast to the compact crystalline pattern of zinc salts observed in the QDP film (Fig. 2(g)) after exposure to the electrolyte. Moreover, the pristine hydrogel films (Fig. S1(d–f)), characterized by a well-aligned wrinkled structure, also deviate significantly from the dense crystal pattern of QDP film. The prepared hydrogel electrolyte and ZnSO4 electrolyte were placed in a freezer at –20 °C. The PP without DMSO was frozen at –20 °C, while the DP and QDP with DMSO remained gelation and liquid at –20 °C (Fig. 2(h, i) and S3). In addition, the 2 M ZnSO4 solution in water-DMSO (4:6, volume ratio) also shows an impressive freezing tolerant behavior than that of electrolytes prepared with DI water (Fig. 2(j, k)). As seen in Fig. 2(l), the QDP exhibits uniform photoluminescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, once again suggesting the successful synthesis and uniform dispersion of QDs in the hydrogel. DMSO as a highly polar non-protonic solvent and excellent antifreeze agent can form a more stable network structure by adjusting the hydrogen bonding between polymer and polymer and polymer and solvent, so that the viscous PVA solution can be transformed into a transparent and firm hard exogen, which participates in and interrupts the hydrogen bonding network of water, and greatly reduces the freezing temperature of the hydrogel, so that the hydrogel can still maintain its transparent gel-like shape at low temperatures, and good mechanical strength and it can be twisted and tensile without rupture (Fig. 2(m, n) and S4) [34–36].

Fig. 3 (a) shows Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of QDs, As can be seen, broad absorption bands at 3000–3600 cm–1 are the stretching vibrations of S−H, O−H, and N−H bonds, and the peak at 1068.4 cm–1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of S−O [37,38]. This indicates the ample amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface, endowing the QDs with good zinc-philic properties. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) surveys of QDs exhibit the presence of C, O, N, and S peaks, once again indicating the rich functional groups within the QDs. In addition, C=O and O−C=O can be observed in the C 1s spectra (Fig. 3(c)), which is consistent with the results of the FTIR spectra. Three peaks deconvoluted from the O 1s spectra of QDs are assigned to C=O at 531.8 eV and C–OH/C–O–C at 530.8 eV (Fig. S5), The high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p show that the peaks at 163.1, 164.3, and 168.3 eV, three peaks, representing S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 and S=O bonding in the thiophene structure, respectively (Fig. 3(d)), which represent the successful doping of S into the carbon backbone of the QDs replacing the carbon atoms to form the thiophene unit [39,40]. N is found in three forms in the QDs at 399.8, 401.7, and 400.5 eV, respectively pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N (Fig. 3(e)), with the highest pyrrole nitrogen content, indicating the presence of more negatively charged N− and O− containing functional groups outside the QDs, which can provide more nucleation sites for Zn2+ to adsorb Zn2+ effectively [41]. Zeta potential measurements were carried out on the QDs (Fig. 3(f)). Owing to the presence of oxygenated/nitride/sulfured groups, the QDs presented an intensely negatively charged state, which echoed with the XPS fine spectra, and ensured the effective adsorption of Zn2+ on the surface of the QDs to reduce the interfacial concentration gradient.

The FTIR spectra of the hydrogel electrolyte (Fig. 3(g)) showed that the broad absorption peak of −OH at 3273.3 cm−1 in PP was blue-shifted with the addition of DMSO, indicating that the addition of DMSO enhanced the hydrogen bonding network [42,43], The strong absorption peak at 1011.0 cm−1 was the expansion vibration peak of the S=O bond in DMSO, and the infrared spectrum of DMSO showed that the absorption peak of S=O bond was redshifted. Owing to the effective interaction between PVA and QDs, the weight loss curves of QDP obviously shift towards the higher temperature region (240–330 °C), as evidenced by the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Fig. S6) up to 800 °C in Ar. As can be seen from the stress-strain diagram of the hydrogel (Fig. 3(h)), the addition of DMSO and QDs improves the tensile strength of the hydrogel electrolyte. The abundant functional groups in the QDs can regulate the movement of PVA segments to form hydrogen bonds, thereby improving the mechanical strength of the electrolyte membrane. The tensile modulus of DP and QDP are 0.31 and 0.27 MPa, which are 15 times that of PP. The DSC curve showed (Fig. 3(i)) that there was a significant crystallization peak of PP at temperatures below 0 °C, the accurate freezing temperature could not be obtained from DSC due to the complex water-polymer interaction between DP and QDP, but the addition of DMSO did hinder the regularized crystallization process of PVA molecular chains to a certain extent, and greatly reduced the crystallization temperature of PVA hydrogels [44].

In order to further study the effects of different hydrogel electrolytes on ZIBs, we assembled the symmetric stainless-steel cell (gel electrolyte -SS/SS@cell) to analyze the electrochemical properties of three gel electrolytes. The electrolyte was analyzed using Linear Scanning Voltammetry (LSV) (Fig. 4 (a)) and the electrochemical stability windows are 2.04, 2.31, and 2.39 of PP, DP, and QDP, respectively. QDP has the highest electrochemical stability window, which meets the needs of ZIBs. Fig. 4(b) shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test, the internal resistance of QDP = 17.4 Ω, which is slightly higher than DP and PP. This is attributed to the enhancement of the hydrogen bonding network in the gel electrolyte by the introduction of QDs, which improves its binding capacity for Zn2+ and weakens the ionic conductivity to some extent. It can be seen from the Chronoamperometry (CA) curve (Fig. 4(c)) that the addition of QDs significantly inhibits the disordered two-dimensional diffusion of Zn2+ and becomes an ordered three-dimensional diffusion, which indicates that the addition of QDs can effectively regulate the deposition of Zn2+ and homogenize the distribution of Zn2+, which further indicates that QDs has an important impact on reducing Zn dendrites formation, The Zn2+ transference number is also evaluated by the QDP-SS/SS@cell and calculated to be about 0.54, which is higher than liquid electrolyte of 0.44.

Symmetrical cell testing was performed on three hydrogel electrolytes to assess the cycling stability of the hydrogel electrolytes. Fig. 4(d) shows the magnification plots of DP-Zn/Zn@cell, QDP-Zn/Zn@cell, and PP-Zn/Zn@cell at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A cm−2 current density, QDP-Zn/Zn@cell exhibits a voltage lag of 0.197 V at a current density of 5 A cm−1, which is lower than that of DP-Zn/Zn@cell and PP-Zn/Zn@cell [45]. As can be seen from the cycle diagram of the symmetrical cell (Fig. 4(e)), the overvoltages of DP-Zn/Zn@cell and QDP-Zn/Zn@cell are always below 250 mV and the voltage distributions were stable over 300 h. The QDP-Zn/Zn@cell exhibited excellent rate performance and low overpotential across all voltage ranges, indicating that the addition of QDs improved the cycle stability and cycle life of the cell [46].

The electrochemical performance of the whole cell at room temperature was tested using a hydrogel electrolyte with an average thickness of 0.8 mm and the PANi electrode was used as the cathode to assemble the whole cell at room temperature. As can be seen from Fig. 4(f), the QDP-Zn/PANi@cell can deliver a high specific capacity of 91.8 mA h g−1, at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. Whereas the DP-Zn/PANi@cell and PP-Zn/PANi@cell can only deliver a specific capacity of 57.8 and 74.3 mAh g−1. The rate diagram of the polyaniline whole cell shows (Fig. 4(g)) that at room temperature, at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A g−1, the capacity of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell is 129.2, 114.8, 91.8, 70.2, 45.6, and 14.4 mAh g−1 showed an optimal discharge specific capacity, with the current density reaching 0.1 and 1.0 A g−1 for the second time, showing a similar capacity to the initial current cycle, which indicates good electrochemical reversibility [47]. As can be seen from the long-term cycling performance at current density of 1 C, the specific capacity of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell is 50.0 mAh g−1 after more than 1500 cycles, and the capacity retention is 71.6 % compared with the 10th cycle (Fig. S7), which shows that QDP-Zn/PANi@cell has high cycle stability, and the coulombic efficiency is maintained at 100% after 2000 cycles. This long lifetime of more than 2000 cycles is due to the fact that the negatively charged QDs effectively reduce the interfacial concentration gradient near the Zn electrode, ensuring sufficient Zn2+ replenishment at high rates, thus exhibiting better cycle durability and cumulative capacity. At high current density of 2C (Fig. 4(i)), the QDP imparts the QDP-Zn/PANi@cell with a high initial capacity, the specific capacity of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell after 2000 cycles was 54.0 mAh g−1, while the specific capacity of DP-Zn/PANi@cell after 2000 cycles was only 42.9 mAh g−1, which may be due to the fact that the ion-conducting capacity of DP-Zn/PANi@cell lower than that of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell, and the Zn2+ deposition is uneven, resulting in a decrease in capacity. It can be seen from the charge-discharge curves at different cycles that the addition of QDs can maintain a relatively stable charge-discharge platform for the assembled Zn/PANi@cell (Fig. 4(j)).

At high current density, there will be an interface concentration gradient on the surface of the Zn electrode, resulting in the uneven deposition of zinc ions and the formation of dendrites. Negatively charged QDs generate a layer of QDs on the surface of the zinc electrode, providing a large number of nucleation sites for Zn2+, decreasing the ion concentration gradient, uniformizing the electric field, accelerating the Zn2+ transport rate, homogenizing Zn deposition, and achieving dendrite-free deposition (Fig. 5 (a)). In order to observe the Zn dendrites deposition more intuitively, we take SEM images of the whole cell Zn electrode and gel electrolyte with 50, 1000, and 2000 cycles at room temperature 1 C (Fig. S8), and we can see that the dendrites on the Zn electrode have been significantly deposited on the Zn electrode after 50 cycles of PP-Zn/PANi@cell, due to the existence of the “tip effect”, the dendrites of PP-Zn/PANi@cell is a mostly upright and sharp flake, which is easy to puncture the electrolyte, short-circuit the batteries and reduce the batteries life (Fig. 5(b)). Due to the presence of DMSO in DP-Zn/PANi@cell, the deposition of Zn2+ plays a certain role in regulating the growth direction of dendrites, and the dendrites formed are mostly parallel to the surface of Zn electrodes (Fig. 5(c)) [48]. However, the addition of QDs effectively reduced the deposition of Zn2+, and after 2000 cycles, the Zn electrode was still relatively flat and had no obvious dendrites (Fig. 5(d)).

As can be seen from the magnification plots at 35 and −10 °C, the capacities of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at 35 °C are 106.9, 79.6, 59.8, 43.9, 24.6, and 3.7 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 5.0 A g−1, and the capacities of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at −10 °C are 82.5, 73.2, 55.8, 32.1, 2.6, and 0.7 mAh g−1 (Fig. 6 (a, b)), the specific capacity of the 35 and −10 °C batteries is reduced compared to room temperature, because both high and low temperatures reduce the ability of Zn2+ to embed and detach between the positive and negative electrodes, resulting in lower internal resistance and reduced specific capacity. From the cycle plots at 35, −10, and −20 °C (Fig. 6(c, e), S9, and S10), it can be observed that QDP-Zn/PANi@cell exhibits good capacity retention at −10 °C, the reversible capacities after 10 and 1000 cycles were 50.4 and 39.3 mAh g−1, respectively, with a capacity retention of 78.0 %. The reversible capacities after 10 and 1000 cycles were 49.7 and 36.3 mAh g−1 at −20 °C. with a capacity retention of 73.0 %, which were higher than those of DP-Zn/PANi@cell and PP-Zn/PANi@cell, coulombic efficiency close to 100 % showing a good capacity retention. It can be observed in the charge-discharge curves of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell (Fig. 6(d, f)), the repeatability of the selected charge-discharge curves for QDP-Zn/PANi@cell is significantly higher than DP-Zn/PANi@cell and PP-Zn/PANi@cell in 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, and 1000 cycles. Moreover, the addition of QDs makes the QDP-Zn/PANi@cell maintain a relatively stable charge-discharge platform at both high and low temperatures. Due to the temperature fluctuations in the cryogenic reaction bath, the corresponding capacity fluctuations can be observed in the capacity efficiency curve. In conclusion, the high cycling stability of QDP-Zn/PANi@cell at low temperatures is attributed to the excellent anti-freezing property of the gel electrolyte and its ability to inhibit the growth of dendritic crystals, which enables them to maintain a high ion transport capacity at low temperatures.

In summary, we prepared a PVA-based hydrogel electrolyte with antifreeze performance, high cycle stability, and inhibition of dendrites growth and side reactions in this work, by introducing negatively charged QDs and DMSO in the PVA-based hydrogel electrolyte, the hydrogen bond between the water molecules in the hydrogel can be broken, its freezing point can be greatly reduced, the electric field on the surface of the Zn electrode is uniformly reduced, the Zn2+ concentration gradient on the Zn surface is reduced, and the Zn is uniformly deposited by adjusting the transport rate of Zn2+, reducing dendrites production. At the same time, the addition of QDs and DMSO greatly improves the mechanical properties of PVA-based hydrogel dielectric, the battery made of this hydrogel electrolyte can work continuously in the temperature range of 35 to −20 °C and can be stable for more than 1000 cycles at room temperature 2 C, stable cycle at low temperature −20 °C and 0.5 C for more than 1000 times, and the capacity retention rate of the battery with QDs is as high as 73.0 % after 1000 cycles. This study provides an idea for the construction of high-performance dendritic-free low-temperature solid-state ZIBs.

Jingyu Du: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao Zhan: Data curation. Yuhuan Xu: Data curation. Kunlan Diao: Data curation. Daohai Zhang: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuhao Qin: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors disclosed no relevant relationships.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China Project (No. 52163001), the Guizhou Minzu University Research Platform Grant (No. GZMUGCZX[2021]01), the Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Program Project Grant (Qiankehe Platform Talents-CXTD[2021]005, Qiankehe Platform Talents-GCC[2022]010-1, Qiankehe Fuqi [2023]001), the Central Guided Local Science and Technology Development Funds Project (Qiankehe Zhong Yindi [2023]035), and the Doctor Startup Fund of Guizhou Minzu University (Grant No. GZMUZK [2024] QD77).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2024.06.023.
